title: How to Work with Triggers During Major Life Transitions
researcherTopic: the body keeps the score, trauma and the brain
metaDescription: Learn how to work with trauma triggers during major life transitions. Understand why they happen and how to navigate them with somatic awareness.
imageDescription: Warm, golden light streams into a quiet room, illuminating the delicate process of someone carefully mending a broken ceramic bowl with gold lacquer, a beautiful representation of kintsugi. The scene is serene and hopeful, focusing on the act of repair and the beauty of imperfection.
finalH2: The Gentle Return: Crafting a New Baseline
htmlContent:
I've sat with hundreds of people as they work through the disorienting territory of major life transitions... a new city, the end of a long relationship, a career change that feels more like a seismic identity shift. What often surfaces in these moments is not the excitement or the fear of the new, but the sudden, inexplicable activation of old wounds, the triggers that we thought we had long since managed or outgrown, which suddenly reappear with an almost startling intensity.

The Architecture of Transition-Based Triggers

A major life transition is at its core an act of deconstruction and reconstruction. We are dismantling a familiar structure of reality - our routines, our roles, our relationships, the very geography of our daily lives... and attempting to build a new one. For a nervous system shaped by complex trauma, this process is not neutral; it is deeply threatening. The system isn t recognize the new apartment or the new job title as by nature safe; it only registers the dissolution of the familiar, the absence of the predictable patterns it once used to move through the world, however imperfectly. This is not a cognitive error. It is the body’s deep, somatic intelligence at work, an intelligence that prioritizes survival above all else, even above our own conscious desires for growth and change.

The Body's Unspoken Archive

The Illusion of a Stable Self

Reorganizing Perception, One Sensation at a Time

The Practice of Being With Yourself

Ultimately, navigating triggers during a major life transition is a practice of learning to be with yourself in a new way. It is the practice of moving from a state of internal division ~ the part of us that is triggered versus the part of us that is judging the trigger... to a state of internal unity. It is the recognition that the trigger is not an enemy to be vanquished, but a messenger from a younger part of ourselves that is in pain and in need of our attention. A transition, with all its uncertainty and upheaval, provides the perfect conditions for this practice. It strips away our familiar coping mechanisms and forces us to find a deeper source of stability within ourselves.

This does not mean that we will no longer be triggered. It means that our relationship to the trigger begins to change. We move from being identified with the trigger to being the witness of the trigger. We learn to create a space of awareness around the trigger, a space that is large enough to hold the intensity of the sensation without being consumed by it. In this space, we can begin to make new choices. We can choose to self-soothe instead of self-abandon. We can choose to reach out for support instead of suffering in silence. What we find here is the slow, steady work of reclaiming our own nervous system, of coming home to ourselves. It is the process from a life defined by the echoes of the past to a life lived in the vibrant, ever-present reality of the now. It is, perhaps, the most important path we will ever take, and one that is beautifully explored in the work on structural dissociation.

Look, I know this is heavy material. And I know there's a part of you that might be thinking "this is too much" or "I can't handle this" or "maybe I should just close this tab." That part is trying to protect you. And that's okay. But I want you to know that you can handle this. You've already handled worse. The fact that you're here, reading this, means your survival system did its job. You made it.

What we're doing here isn't about reliving the past. It's about understanding it well enough to stop being controlled by it. There's a difference between being trapped in a memory and looking at it from a safe distance. You get to choose how close you get. You get to choose when to step back. That choice - that agency - is itself a form of healing.

Frequently Asked Questions

How can I tell the difference between a normal stress response to a big change and a trauma trigger?

A normal stress response is typically proportionate to the current situation and subsides as you adapt to the new circumstances. A trauma trigger, however, often feels disproportionately intense, bringing with it a cascade of emotions, sensations, and even flashbacks that are clearly rooted in past experiences rather than the present moment. It feels less like stress and more like a full-body hijacking, a return to an old state of emergency.

A resource that helps make polyvagal theory practical is The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link).

Is it possible to go through a major transition without being triggered at all?

For a nervous system with a history of complex trauma, it's highly unlikely, and perhaps not even desirable. Triggers are not a sign of failure, but an indication that something old is ready to be met with new awareness. The goal is not to avoid triggers, but to build the capacity to be with them in a way that leads to integration and healing, transforming them from moments of crisis into opportunities for striking self-reclamation.

The work of healing is not about arriving at a place where the past no longer exists. It is about arriving at a place where the past no longer dictates the present. The transition is not the obstacle; it is the path.

From Insight to Integration

There is a vast and often treacherous territory that lies between gaining a new insight and truly integrating it into the fabric of one’s being. We can read every book on trauma, we can intellectually grasp the elegant models of polyvagal theory or the mechanics of somatic experiencing, and yet find ourselves caught in the same reactive loops when a trigger hits. It turns out to be because information is not shift. The real work of healing happens when an intellectual concept ~ a ‘should’ or a ‘know’... makes the long road down from the neocortex into the felt sense of the body, becoming an embodied reality rather than just a good idea. A major life transition is an accelerator for this process, as it relentlessly exposes the gap between what we understand and how we actually respond under pressure.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link).

Integration is not a glamorous or heroic act. It is the quiet, unsexy, and deeply repetitive work of choosing a new response, over and over again. It is the thousandth time you notice the familiar clench of anxiety and consciously choose to soften your belly and deepen your breath. It is the moment you feel the pull of an old, self-abandoning pattern and instead choose to stay with yourself, offering a silent acknowledgment of the pain. I can stay here. I will not leave myself.’ That becomes how we move beyond simply managing our triggers and begin to metabolize them, digesting the stored survival energy and transforming it into fuel for our present-day lives. It is the alchemy of turning the lead of old trauma into the gold of embodied presence, a process that is less about grand epiphanies and more about the steady accumulation of small, conscious moments of turning toward ourselves with kindness and care.

This process is not about perfection; it is about direction. There will be moments, even years into the work, when we are completely hijacked by a trigger, when we revert to old patterns, when we feel as though all our progress has been lost. These moments are not evidence of our failure. They are simply evidence of the depth of the original wound and the tenacity of the nervous system’s programming. It is to notice the detour, to feel the sting of the old pain, and then to gently, firmly, guide ourselves back to the path. What we find here is the heart of the practice ~ not the avoidance of triggers, but the ever-deepening capacity to return to ourselves after we have been pulled away. It is in this constant returning that we build the true resilience that allows us to find your way through the inevitable transitions of life not as a series of crises to be survived, but as a continuous unfolding of our own becoming.

Know what I mean when I say "trigger stacking"? It's when multiple small triggers pile up throughout a day until your system hits capacity. None of them alone would be enough to send you over the edge. But together, they're a tsunami. The bad sleep. The rude email. The loud noise. The crowded store. Each one takes a little more from your window of tolerance until there's nothing left.

This is why you sometimes "explode" over something tiny. It wasn't the tiny thing. It was everything that came before it. Your nervous system had been absorbing hits all day, and that last one was just the one that broke through the dam. The people around you see the explosion and think you're overreacting to a spilled coffee. They don't see the hundred micro-triggers that preceded it.

Here's what helps. Tracking. Not in a obsessive way, but in a curious way. Start noticing the small activations throughout your day. The ones you usually push through. The ones you tell yourself "it's fine, I'm fine." Because each one of those is a withdrawal from your nervous system's bank account. And if you don't make deposits - rest, safety, connection, pleasure - you'll hit zero before the day is done. Every time.

I know, I know.

A breathing tool that helps slow the exhale for nervous system regulation is Breathing Necklace - Shift (paid link).

The Gentle Return: Crafting a New Baseline

Every major life transition is a significant invitation to establish a new baseline for our nervous system. The old baseline, often calibrated to a state of high alert and subtle brace, was a brilliant adaptation to past circumstances. It kept us safe in environments where danger was real or perceived. The work, then, is not to simply endure the transition but to consciously use it as an opportunity to teach the body a new definition of safety, a new resting state. What emerges is a delicate and deeply personal process, one that unfolds not through force of will but through a steady application of gentle, consistent, and attuned presence.

Crafting this new baseline is a somatic, not a cognitive, project. It involves creating small, repeated experiences of safety and ease that, over time, accumulate and begin to outweigh the body's deeply ingrained habit of vigilance. It might look like finding a particular corner of your new home that feels calming and intentionally spending five minutes there each morning, simply feeling the sensation of being held and at ease. I've worked with clients who have found immense resource in sound, using specific frequencies or calming music to provide a sense of auditory safety in an unfamiliar environment. These are not distractions or coping mechanisms in the traditional sense. They are deliberate, targeted inputs into the nervous system, designed to provide it with the raw data of safety, moment by moment by moment.

This process requires a radical shift in our relationship with ourselves. We must become the patient, loving parent to our own inner world that we likely never had. When the inevitable wave of a trigger arrives - the sudden panic in the grocery store, the inexplicable sadness on a sunny afternoon ~ the practice is to meet it not with frustration or a demand that it go away, but with a quiet, internal whisper: ‘I am here with you. It is intense, but it is not the whole truth of this moment.’ This is the essence of what it means to truly be with yourself. It is the steady, unwavering commitment to providing the internal holding environment that allows these old, powerful energies to finally move through and release their grip. It is in this gentle, persistent returning to a state of internal safety that we don't just survive the transition; we allow it to transform us from the inside out, building a foundation of resilience that will serve us for the rest of our lives.

A grounding tool that many find helpful during anxious moments is Spiky Sensory Ring Set (paid link).

The body does not care about your timeline. It does not care about your plans or your preferences or your carefully constructed narrative about how healing should look. Everything else is negotiation.
